worth_it_explanation: Buyer and seat controls decide which demand reaches the auction, so they sit directly between a publisher's inventory and its fill. A blocklist that grows over time, or one copied from another property without review, can suppress demand that would have bid. The check is a review of the buyer allowlist, blocklist, and category blocks a publisher already maintains on TripleLift, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can adjust and measure.
intro: Buyer and seat controls decide which demand a publisher accepts on TripleLift, the supply-side platform (SSP) carrying the inventory into the open auction. These controls include the buyer allowlist and blocklist, blocked advertiser domains, and blocked content categories that filter the bids reaching each impression. This subcategory is about whether those controls are maintained on purpose, with each block traceable to a reason, rather than accumulated over time. These checks confirm that the blocklist reflects current intent and that the blocks in place are not so broad that they cut off legitimate demand the publisher meant to keep.

audit_checks.title: Buyer and seat blocklists are not maintained with a reason on record
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A blocklist that no one reviews drifts away from current intent. Blocks added for a campaign that ended, or carried over from another property, keep filtering demand long after the reason expired, so the publisher loses bids without a record of why each buyer was excluded.

educate.base:
TripleLift is a supply-side platform (SSP) and exchange that carries a publisher's inventory into the programmatic auction, including its native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats. Because TripleLift is compatible with Prebid, the header-bidding framework, the buyer and seat controls a publisher manages on it map to the same OpenRTB blocking fields the open auction uses. A buyer blocklist excludes specific demand partners or advertiser domains, and a category block excludes whole content categories from bidding on the inventory.

These controls earn their keep when each block traces back to a current reason. A publisher may block an advertiser domain after a brand-safety incident, block a category that conflicts with a direct-sold sponsorship, or exclude a buyer that delivered creative the publisher did not want. Each of those is a deliberate choice. The problem starts when the list keeps growing and nobody prunes it. A block added for a campaign that ended six months ago keeps filtering demand. A list copied from another property carries that property's exclusions, which may not fit this one.

The OpenRTB specification that TripleLift and Prebid both build on defines the fields these controls use. Blocked advertiser domains travel in the 'badv' field, blocked advertiser categories in the 'bcat' field, and the standard lists those alongside the seat-level controls that decide which buyers transact. A maintained blocklist is one where the publisher can name, for each entry, why it is there and whether the reason still holds.

A few signs point to a blocklist that is accumulating rather than maintained:
  • The blocklist has entries the publisher cannot explain or tie to a current reason
  • No review date or owner is recorded for the list, so nobody is responsible for pruning it
  • Blocks were copied from another property or inherited from a prior team without a check against this property's needs
You may want to confirm that each block on the list still serves a current purpose. One approach is to read the buyer blocklist, blocked domains, and blocked categories with the publisher, record the reason for each, and remove the entries whose reason has expired.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, category blocks often protect direct-sold sponsorships or keep sensitive content categories off editorial inventory. You may want to confirm that each category block still matches a live sponsorship or a current editorial standard. A block left in place after a sponsorship ends keeps filtering demand the publisher could now accept.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, advertiser-domain blocks may exclude direct competitors of a retail partner. You may want to confirm that each competitor block still reflects a current partnership. A block tied to a partnership that has lapsed cuts off demand without a reason that still holds.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed configuration documentation for buyer and seat controls is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the buyer blocklist, blocked advertiser domains, and blocked categories on the TripleLift account, and record the reason and review status for each entry.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift publisher console
steps: Open the buyer or demand controls section of the TripleLift account and list the active buyer allowlist and blocklist entries.
List the blocked advertiser domains and blocked content categories applied to the inventory.
For each entry, ask the publisher to state the reason it was added and whether that reason still holds.
Record any entry the publisher cannot explain, any list with no owner or review date, and any block copied from another property without a check against this one.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each buyer, domain, and category block alongside its stated reason and review status. A blocklist where every entry traces to a current reason and the list has an owner and a review date passes. A blocklist carrying entries the publisher cannot explain, or one with no owner or review process, is a fail recorded with the unexplained entries listed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each buyer, domain, and category block with its reason and review status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} maintains buyer and seat blocklists on TripleLift without a recorded reason or review process for each entry. A blocklist that no one prunes drifts away from current intent, so blocks added for a campaign that ended, or carried over from another property, keep filtering demand long after the reason expired. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the buyer blocklist, blocked advertiser domains, and blocked categories with the publisher, recording the reason for each entry, and removing the blocks whose reason no longer holds. After the review, assign an owner and a review date so the list stays tied to current intent.

audit_checks.title: Blocks are broad enough to suppress legitimate demand and fill
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A category or domain block wider than the concern it addresses removes demand the publisher meant to keep. A whole-category block applied to stop one advertiser, or a blanket block across all inventory when only one placement was at issue, lowers bid density and fill on inventory that had no problem.

educate.base:
A block on TripleLift, the supply-side platform (SSP) carrying the inventory, does its job when it is as narrow as the concern it addresses. The trouble comes when a block is wider than it needs to be. Blocking a whole content category to keep out one advertiser within it removes every other buyer in that category too. Blocking an advertiser domain across all inventory, when the concern was one placement, cuts that advertiser off everywhere. Each of these stops more demand than the publisher meant to stop.

The cost shows up as lower bid density and lower fill. Every buyer or category removed from the auction is one fewer source of competition on the impression. Fewer bids per impression means a lower chance the impression clears at all, and a lower clearing price when it does. The auction has less pressure pushing the price up. A block that removes a real problem is worth that cost. A block that removes far more than the problem trades away fill and revenue for protection the publisher did not need at that width.

The OpenRTB specification that TripleLift and Prebid build on supports blocking at the level of the specific advertiser domain ('badv') and the specific category ('bcat'), so a publisher can scope a block to the exact concern rather than reaching for the widest available control. The audit question is whether the blocks in place are scoped to their reason or whether a broad block is doing work that a narrow one should do.

A few signs point to blocks that are broader than their reason:
  • A whole content category is blocked when the concern was a single advertiser within it
  • A block applies across all inventory when the reason involved only one placement or format
  • Bid density or fill is noticeably lower on inventory covered by a broad block than on comparable inventory without one
You may want to confirm that each block is scoped to the concern that prompted it. One approach is to compare bid density and fill on blocked inventory against comparable unblocked inventory, then narrow any block that is suppressing more demand than its reason requires.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a brand-safety concern about one advertiser can lead to blocking an entire category across editorial inventory. You may want to scope the block to the specific advertiser domain instead, so the rest of that category can still bid on inventory the concern never touched.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a competitor block tied to one retail partnership can end up applied across all inventory rather than the placements the partnership covers. You may want to limit the block to the relevant placements so the rest of the inventory keeps the demand the wider block would remove.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed configuration documentation for scoping buyer and category blocks is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the width of each block against the reason it addresses, and compare bid density and fill on blocked inventory against comparable unblocked inventory.

triplelift_console.label: TripleLift publisher console
steps: Open the buyer and demand controls and list each category block, domain block, and buyer exclusion along with the inventory it covers.
For each block, record whether it is scoped to a specific advertiser domain and placement or applied at the category or whole-inventory level.
Ask the publisher what concern prompted each block, and note any case where the block is wider than that concern.
Where reporting is available, compare bid density and fill on inventory under a broad block against comparable inventory without one, and record any drop that points to suppressed demand.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block, the inventory it covers, the concern it addresses, and the bid density and fill on the affected inventory where available. Blocks scoped to the specific advertiser or placement that prompted them pass. A category-wide or inventory-wide block applied to a concern that involved a single advertiser, format, or placement, especially where bid density or fill is lower than on comparable unblocked inventory, is a fail recorded with the over-broad blocks listed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block, its scope, the concern it addresses, and the bid density and fill on the affected inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies buyer and category blocks on TripleLift that are broader than the concern that prompted them, which suppresses legitimate demand. A category or domain block wider than its reason removes buyers the publisher meant to keep, so bid density and fill fall on inventory that had no problem. {{voice_recommend}} scoping each block to the specific advertiser domain, format, or placement that prompted it, using the narrow controls the OpenRTB standard supports rather than the widest available block. After narrowing the blocks, compare bid density and fill against comparable unblocked inventory to confirm the demand returned without reopening the original concern.
